design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate diverse level designs with varying mechanics (run-and-gun, platforming, puzzle-solving, trivia, isometric shooting) to progress toward rescuing Princess What's-Her-Name from Psy-Crow
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Genre and mechanic surprise—each level introduces radically different gameplay systems, requiring rapid player adaptation and breaking expectations established by previous levels
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as an irreverent, comedic earthworm hero in a chaotic, surreal world where standard platformer rules don't always apply; experiencing absurdist humor through gameplay subversion
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Radical mechanical diversity within a single game—rejecting the formula-repetition typical of platformers by treating each level as an experiment in a different genre (shooter, puzzle, trivia game, isometric adventure, race). The ending twist (all characters revealed as cows) demonstrates commitment to absurdist humor over narrative payoff. Sidekick mechanic (Snott as swing/parachute) and weapon variety (auto-aiming, screen-clearing 'Barn Blaster') are non-standard for 1995 platformers.

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High—diverse level mechanics allow different players to find difficulty in different places; some levels emphasize pattern memorization (run-and-gun sections), others reward exploration or puzzle-solving. Mechanic variety may frustrate or delight depending on player preference.

progression.upgrade_system: Power-ups collected within levels (chip butty increases suit power to 200%, gun variety including auto-aiming and screen-clearing 'Barn Blaster')
progression.unlock_mechanism: Level-by-level progression; beating one level advances to the next with no branching paths documented
replayability_hooks: Diverse level mechanics encourage replaying specific levels for different gameplay experiences
Multiple weapon types reward experimentation on replay
Absurdist narrative twist (cow reveal) subverts expectation and provides comedic replayability incentive

world_building_techniques: Level design as narrative (levels like 'Villi People' with intestinal passages create grotesque world implied by the premise)
Character integration (Snott as companion with in-level mechanical purpose deepens character presence)
Absurdist humor in level titles and mechanics ('Inflated Head', 'Level Ate', 'See Jim Run Run Jim Run') that build comic tone
Narrative subversion through gameplay (trivia level in 'Villi People' with nonsensical questions breaks fourth wall)
Meta-twist ending (characters as cows) delivered as mechanical/visual reveal rather than dialogue

boss_design.philosophy: Varied boss encounters matching level-specific mechanics rather than standard boss fights. Final encounter is a race, not combat—subverting boss-fight conventions. Major Mucus encountered in isometric shooter context ('The Flyin' King'). Evil the Cat appears in 'Inflated Head' as an environmental hazard integrated into the balloon-floating mechanic.
notable_bosses: Psy-Crow (final encounter as race/obstacle course, not standard combat)
Major Mucus (isometric shooter context, balloon defense mechanic)
Evil the Cat (integrated into 'Inflated Head' level as attack pattern within floating mechanic)

faction_indicators: Princess What's-Her-Name (rescue objective, comedically unnamed, subverted as cow)
danger_signals: Exploding sheep as environmental hazards ('Villi People')
Sharp objects threatening balloon form ('Inflated Head')
Intestinal passage hazards and embedded obstacles
Enemy variety across different mechanical contexts
environmental_storytelling: Intestinal passage design ('Villi People') creates body-horror implied world
Level titles as comedic hints ('Level Ate' = 'levitate', 'Inflated Head' describes mechanic)
Isometric spaceship/rocket environment in 'The Flyin' King'
Absurdist set pieces (balloon version of Jim, blind cave salamander disguise) establish tone through environment
